hundreds.desc: Strict mode, annotations, generics, utility types.
hundreds.label: Type System & Type Safety

tens.desc: Strict mode, annotations, interfaces, generics, utility types.
tens.label: Type System & Type Safety

rule.desc: Enable `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json` for all projects.
Annotate return types on exported functions and public methods.
Prefer `interface` for object shapes that may be extended; use `type` for unions, intersections, mapped types, and primitives.
Use generics for reusable components that work with multiple types. Constrain type parameters with `extends` when possible.
Use built-in utility types (`Partial`, `Required`, `Pick`, `Omit`, `Record`, `Readonly`) instead of manual type construction.
Use literal types and `as const` assertions for values that must be specific constants.
Use discriminated unions with a shared literal property for type-safe variants.
Use branded types to distinguish semantically different values that share a primitive representation.
Use template literal types for string patterns that follow a known format.
Prefer union types or `as const` objects over TypeScript enums. Use enums only when numeric reverse mapping is required.

hundreds.desc: Modules, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.
hundreds.label: Code Organization & Modules

tens.desc: Module structure, exports, barrel files, dependency direction.
tens.label: Code Organization & Modules

rule.desc: Use ES modules (`import`/`export`) exclusively. Avoid CommonJS `require`/`module.exports`.
Export only what consumers need. Use named exports over default exports.
Use barrel files (`index.ts`) sparingly for package boundaries. Avoid deep barrel nesting.
Use `import type` for imports used only in type positions.
Dependencies flow inward: infrastructure depends on domain, never the reverse.
Use path aliases in `tsconfig.json` for cross-cutting imports instead of deep relative paths.

tens.desc: Readonly, const assertions, pure functions, immutable patterns.
tens.label: Immutability & Functional Patterns

rule.desc: Mark properties, parameters, and arrays as `readonly` by default. Remove `readonly` only when mutation is intentional.
Use `as const` for literal objects and arrays that should not change.
Prefer pure functions: same input produces same output, no side effects.
Return new objects or arrays instead of mutating inputs. Use spread syntax or utility methods.
Prefer `map`, `filter`, `reduce`, and other array methods over imperative loops for data transformations.
Avoid module-level mutable state. Use dependency injection or factory functions instead.

hundreds.desc: Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types.
hundreds.label: Error Handling

tens.desc: Custom errors, exhaustive checks, Result types, type guards.
tens.label: Error Handling

rule.desc: Use exhaustive checks with `never` to ensure all union members are handled.
Extend `Error` for domain-specific errors. Include structured context, not just message strings.
Narrow caught errors with `instanceof` or type guards. Never assume `catch (e)` is an `Error`.
Consider Result/Either types for operations where failure is expected and should be handled by callers.
Use type guard functions (`is` return type) and assertion functions (`asserts` return type) for complex type narrowing.
Validate and parse external data at system boundaries using runtime validation (e.g., Zod, io-ts, Valibot).
Use `async`/`await` over raw Promise chains (`.then`/`.catch`).
Use `Promise.all` for independent concurrent operations; use `Promise.allSettled` when partial failure is acceptable.
Accept `AbortSignal` for cancellable async operations. Propagate signals through the call chain.
Use typed event emitters or observable patterns instead of untyped string-based events.
Use `for await...of` with async iterables for streaming data. Prefer async generators over manual iterator implementation.

tens.desc: Type testing, mocking, assertions, test structure.
tens.label: Testing & Tooling

rule.desc: Write type-level tests for complex generics and utility types using `Expect`/`Equal` helpers or `tsd`.
Use typed mocking utilities that preserve interface contracts. Avoid `as any` in test doubles.
Structure tests with Arrange/Act/Assert (or Given/When/Then). One logical assertion group per test.
Use descriptive test names that state the behavior and context, not the implementation.
Mock external systems (HTTP, DB, clock, filesystem). Do not mock value objects, utility functions, or the module under test.
Use `it.each` or `describe.each` for behavior with multiple inputs. Avoid copy-pasted test bodies.

hundreds.desc: Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak types.
hundreds.label: Type Safety Violations

tens.desc: Any abuse, unsafe assertions, weak typing, ts-ignore.
tens.label: Type Safety Violations

rule.desc: Using `any` to bypass type checking instead of proper types.
Using `@ts-ignore` or `@ts-expect-error` to suppress type errors instead of fixing them.
Using the non-null assertion operator (`!`) instead of proper null checks.
Using `as Type` to force a type without runtime validation.
Using plain objects (`Record<string, T>`) as maps when `Map<K, V>` is more appropriate.
Functions with inferred `any` return type due to missing annotation or unsafe operations.
Using bare `string` for parameters that accept only specific values.
Using a single interface with optional fields to model variants instead of a discriminated union.
Using TypeScript `namespace` for code organization instead of ES modules.
Excessive function overloads when a union parameter or generic would be clearer.

hundreds.desc: Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export issues.
hundreds.label: Module & Import Problems

tens.desc: Circular deps, barrel file pitfalls, re-export problems.
tens.label: Module & Import Problems

rule.desc: Circular module dependencies that cause runtime initialization errors or `undefined` imports.
Barrel files (`index.ts`) that re-export everything from every module, causing bundle bloat and slow IDE performance.
Modules that execute side effects on import (register globals, modify prototypes, start servers).
Using `export * from` which leaks implementation details and creates name collisions.
Default exports that lose their name binding, causing inconsistent names across imports.

hundreds.desc: Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.
hundreds.label: Async & Runtime Hazards

tens.desc: Unhandled promises, callback mixing, shared mutable state.
tens.label: Async & Runtime Hazards

rule.desc: Calling an async function without `await` or an attached rejection handler, leaving the Promise to reject unobserved.
Mixing callback-style and Promise-style APIs in the same flow.
Passing `async` functions to event handlers or callbacks that expect synchronous `void` return.
Sharing mutable state between async operations without coordination.
Wrapping an existing Promise in `new Promise()` unnecessarily.
Sequentially awaiting independent Promises instead of running them concurrently.
